paragraphs.body: John breathed in through his nose and out through his mouth with a steady cadence, huffing and puffing as his feet pounded against the asphalt below him one at a time as he pumped his arms, propelling the rest of his body forward. He learned the trick from his middle school track coach who told him, “The faster your arms move, the faster your legs move to keep up.” John wasn’t so sure this was grounded in scientific evidence, but it certainly seemed to work for him as he approached the largest hill on his route.

annotations.comment: Don’t need this phrase (no other way to run!)

paragraphs.body: His quads and calves screamed with exhaustion. He tried to ignore their cries and the seductive song of the sirens, telling him to quit, just as a courageous Greek adventurer did some 2,000 years ago. You know you want to. Nobody is making you do this. Just stop for a minute. The angel on his shoulder swooped into his defense. No, you got this. One step at a time. One foot in front of the other. Focus on the breath. Keep moving forward. John listened to his better angels and continued.

annotations.comment: Love this idea of the sirens as extremely tired muscles. :)

paragraphs.body: The marathon he had signed up for (perhaps foolishly in hindsight) was less than a month away, and quitting now meant making his life even harder during the race. He knew how important these long runs were to his training plan. Excitement—or maybe it was dread—gave him an extra push as the top of the hill finally came into view. The last moments of sunlight waned, and dusk began to set in.

annotations.comment: Lovely writing. You might add in some indication about why he feels he must actually compete in this marathon. (Or why he signed up for it even as he isn’t seemingly that excited for it?)
annotations.context_left: The marathon he had signed up for (perhaps foolishly
annotations.context_right: hindsight) was less than a month away, and quitting now meant making his life even harder during th

paragraphs.body: As he crested the hill, the lactic acid burning in his muscles began to dissipate. He sighed, exhaling powerfully, as if blowing out stubborn birthday candles. Gravity kicked in, pulling John downhill. The muscle fibers in his legs relaxed, and the side stitch creeping up his rib cage subsided. Mentally, his sanity returned. The ground leveled out. He found his rhythm again. One Mississippi. Phhhh. Two Mississippi. Phhhh. And his mind began to wander.

annotations.comment: Again, a lovely description. As someone who finds running downhill as tiresome as running uphill, I was surprised, but I buy it.

paragraphs.body: Running had been a distraction—an escape. John started after college, where he had about as successful a career as one could have as a D3 soccer player. All-American center midfielder his senior year. All-academic team four straight years. A Sweet 16 appearance in the NCAA tournament his sophomore year. He was proud of all of it—maybe not as much as his father.

John’s father had lived vicariously through him, investing in every practice, every game, and every disappointment. Soccer was their relationship. John feared that quitting would strip away the only thread binding them together. Fear of his father’s reaction. Fear of losing the only thing holding their relationship together. Fear of losing another parent.

annotations.comment: It’s here that we start to see what the conflict in the story is. I wonder if you can set this up a bit more earlier — perhaps he thinks about this while running, or is glad to NOT be able to think about it while running.

paragraphs.body: The doubts festered, creeping up on him like the ache in his calves. He remembered the early mornings, the dew still clinging to the grass as he sprinted up and down the field with his father barking orders. It wasn’t just about soccer—it was about winning, about toughness, about being a man. He remembered the strain in his father’s eyes when he’d fallen short. Disappointment disguised as tough love. He wondered if his father had ever realized how much John’s own love for the game had been tainted by fear. Or how many times he stayed late after practice not for the thrill of the sport but because he was afraid to come home.

annotations.comment: Powerful. I don’t quite buy that he doesn’t remember when his parents divorced. Maybe not when they split up. Or maybe not precisely when it happened, but generally, yes.

paragraphs.body: John couldn’t remember when his parents got divorced—just that it was taboo around his father. Smiths didn’t talk about their problems; they buried them deep down and adopted unhealthy coping mechanisms to quell them. He could still picture his mother’s face—deep brown eyes, a pearly white smile, freckles scattered across her nose, and straight brown hair glowing like the actresses in a Dove commercial. But the memory was fading. She left when John was three, vanishing without so much as a note. Just packed the station wagon, dropped him at daycare, and drove off, never to be seen again.

annotations.comment: This paragraph is working slightly against itself — it feels like the key fact is that the mom left, not that the parents divorced, right?
annotations.context_left: out so much as a not

paragraphs.body: The hater’s voice clawed its way back in. Why are you doing this? You don’t even like running. You just swapped one punishment for another.
He pushed forward, legs burning anew. The old voice—his father’s—drilled into him: Push through it. Winners never quit.

But the doubts piled up. What happens when the ball stops rolling? When I can’t run anymore? What if I never make him proud? What if I can’t figure out who I am without a jersey on my back? What if I fail at this too?

He remembered the sleepless nights in college, lying awake with a textbook he wasn’t reading, listening for his father’s praise in words never spoken. The phantom ache of his mother’s absence was ever-present. The memory was a shadow in the back of his mind—a flicker of love abandoned. And now, miles from anywhere, chasing a finish line no one was watching, John wondered if he’d spent a lifetime running from something he could never outrun.

annotations.comment: I wonder if you want to consider some hardship that’s happening to him as he runs — an old injury he’s pushing through or something other just the usual discomfort.
Can you tie this to the dad a bit more directly? Maybe mailing him a copy of a grade, etc. and not hearing back the praise he wants.

paragraphs.body: His stride faltered, breath hitching. He wanted to stop. God, he wanted to stop. Then, something cracked inside him—a voice, soft but fierce, broke through: It doesn’t matter if he’s proud of you. It matters if you’re proud of yourself.
John surged forward, legs screaming but alive. He would finish this run—not for his father, but for himself. Tomorrow, I am going to call him. It was time to stop running away from his problems.

annotations.comment: Ah, lovely. I think “tomorrow I’m going to call him” resonates more if we understand (as I think you mean) that he will call him and tell him he’s not going to play soccer anymore? I really liked this story, Reza. It’s composed with care — a good example of how helpful focus on a particular moment can be in shaping a story. I think you can help yourself a bit by thinking through where the relationship with the dad is just before the run — has he just called or sent a text that set John off in some way? Or did something else propel him to this run? Or perhaps something happens on it before his epiphany — some realization that he has been in pain for the last 12 years. It’s possible the mom might be involved, that perhaps she reached out to her son for the first time in years, etc. I like the idea of the story happening mostly internally, but I think you need more of an externalization — to a particular event — to make it work. Very nicely done! Happy revising!
